content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 7 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 7 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 7:1 -- "not to have sexual relations with a woman / not to touch a woman"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "concerning the [things] about which you wrote: 'It is good for a man not to touch a woman'" | haptesthai as "touch" (literal); preserves Corinthian slogan in quotes |
| LOGOS | "Now concerning the matters about which you wrote: 'It is good for a man not to have sexual relations with a woman'" | haptesthai as "have sexual relations" (interpretive) |
| PNEUMA | "Now regarding the questions you asked in your letter. Yes, it is good to abstain from sexual relations" | Drops quotation marks; adds "Yes"; "abstain from" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Have sexual relations" correctly interprets the euphemistic use of haptesthai ("to touch") in this context. EMET's "touch" is literal but potentially misleading in English (mere physical contact). The critical issue is PNEUMA's treatment: by dropping the quotation marks and adding "Yes," PNEUMA presents this as Paul's own affirmation rather than a Corinthian slogan he is responding to. Most scholars recognize this as a Corinthian position Paul is quoting, and the quotation marks must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's removal of quotation marks flagged as a significant interpretive error that misrepresents Paul's position.

---

## 7:2 -- "temptation to sexual immorality / because there is so much sexual immorality"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of the sexual immoralities, let each [man] have the own wife" | Plural porneias; "let each have" (imperative) |
| LOGOS | "But because of the temptation to sexual immorality, each man should have his own wife" | Singular + "temptation to" |
| PNEUMA | "But because there is so much sexual immorality, each man should have his own wife" | "so much" quantifies; drops "temptation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Because of the temptation to sexual immorality" captures the dia + accusative (indicating cause/reason) and the plural porneias (various sexual sins) as a general category. PNEUMA's "so much" quantifies what Paul leaves general. EMET's plural "sexual immoralities" is lexically precise but unusual in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:3 -- "conjugal rights / obligation / sexual needs"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let the husband render to the wife the obligation" | opheile as "obligation" |
| LOGOS | "The husband should give to his wife her conjugal rights" | opheile as "conjugal rights" |
| PNEUMA | "The husband should fulfill his wife's sexual needs" | opheile as "sexual needs" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Conjugal rights" translates opheile (what is owed, obligation) in its marital context with appropriate formality. EMET's "obligation" is too generic. PNEUMA's "sexual needs" shifts from the language of duty/rights to the language of desire, which changes Paul's framework -- he is speaking of what is owed, not what is desired.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's shift from duty to desire noted.

---

## 7:5 -- "devote yourselves to prayer / give yourselves more completely to prayer"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that you may devote [yourselves] to prayer, and again be together, lest the Satan tempt you" | scholazo as "devote" |
| LOGOS | "that you may devote yourselves to prayer; but then come together again, so that Satan may not tempt you because of your lack of self-control" | Same core; smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "so you can give yourselves more completely to prayer. Afterward, you should come together again so that Satan won't be able to tempt you because of your lack of self-control" | "more completely" adds intensity; "Afterward" for temporal marker |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "more completely" adds a comparative not in the Greek -- scholazo simply means to devote time to something, not to do so more intensely. The other differences are minor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 7:9 -- "burn with passion / burn with lust / burn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for it is better to marry than to burn" | pyrousthai as "burn" (no object specified) |
| LOGOS | "For it is better to marry than to burn with passion" | Adds "with passion" |
| PNEUMA | "It's better to marry than to burn with lust" | "lust" instead of "passion" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's bare "burn" is literal but ambiguous in English (burn with what? in hell?). Adding "with passion" (LOGOS) clarifies the metaphor. PNEUMA's "lust" carries stronger negative connotations than the Greek warrants -- Paul is describing unfulfilled sexual desire, not inherently sinful desire. "Passion" is more neutral.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:10-11 -- "charge/command" and "separate/leave"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I charge -- not I but the Lord -- a wife is not to separate from [the] husband...and a husband is not to divorce [the] wife" | parangello as "charge"; chorizo as "separate" (wife); aphiemi as "divorce" (husband) |
| LOGOS | "I give this charge (not I, but the Lord): the wife should not separate from her husband...and the husband should not divorce his wife" | Same terms in smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "I have a command that comes not from me, but from the Lord. A wife must not leave her husband...And the husband must not leave his wife" | parangello as "command"; chorizo and aphiemi both as "leave"; "must not" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The key issue is PNEUMA's use of "leave" for both chorizo ("separate") and aphiemi ("divorce/send away"). Paul uses two different Greek verbs -- chorizo for the wife's action and aphiemi for the husband's -- reflecting ancient legal realities (wives could separate; husbands could formally divorce). PNEUMA's flattening both to "leave" loses this distinction. LOGOS preserves it with "separate" and "divorce."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's conflation of chorizo and aphiemi noted.

---

## 7:14 -- "made holy / sanctified / brings holiness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the unbelieving husband has been sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife has been sanctified by the brother" | hagiazo as "sanctified"; "brother" for adelphos |
| LOGOS | "the unbelieving husband is made holy because of his wife, and the unbelieving wife is made holy because of her husband" | hagiazo as "made holy" |
| PNEUMA | "the believing wife brings holiness to her marriage, and the believing husband brings holiness to his marriage" | Restructures: the believer "brings holiness" to the marriage rather than the unbeliever "being sanctified" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's restructuring fundamentally changes the grammatical subject and object. In the Greek, the unbeliever is the one who "has been sanctified" (passive) -- it is something that happens to the unbeliever through the believing spouse. PNEUMA makes the believer the active agent who "brings holiness to the marriage," which shifts the focus from a status conferred on the person to an influence exerted on the institution.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's restructuring flagged as a significant change of meaning.

---

## 7:15 -- "not enslaved / not bound / no longer bound"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The brother or the sister is not enslaved in such [cases]. But in peace God has called you" | douloo as "enslaved" (passive) |
| LOGOS | "In such cases the brother or sister is not enslaved. God has called you to peace" | Same; "to peace" |
| PNEUMA | "In such cases the believing husband or wife is no longer bound to the other, for God has called you to live in peace" | "no longer bound" for douloo; adds "to the other" and "to live" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Not enslaved" (ou dedoulotai) is more precise than "no longer bound" (PNEUMA). Paul chose douloo (enslave) rather than deo (bind), which is the word he uses in v.39 for the marriage bond. The distinction matters theologically: in v.15 Paul says the believer is not enslaved (to the obligation of maintaining the marriage), not necessarily that the marriage bond is dissolved. PNEUMA's "no longer bound" conflates the two concepts.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The douloo vs. deo distinction noted as theologically significant.

---

## 7:21 -- "avail yourself of the opportunity / take it"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But if indeed you are able to become free, rather make use [of it]" | mallon chresai -- ambiguous directive |
| LOGOS | "But if you can gain your freedom, avail yourself of the opportunity" | Takes "it" as freedom |
| PNEUMA | "but if you get a chance to be free, take it" | Same interpretation, more colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This verse contains a famous ambiguity: does mallon chresai mean "make use of the opportunity for freedom" or "make use of your present condition (slavery) all the more"? Most modern scholars favor the former, as does SOPHIA. LOGOS's "avail yourself of the opportunity" is clear. PNEUMA's "take it" is equivalent but less formal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The interpretive ambiguity noted per EMET.

---

## 7:25 -- "betrothed / virgins / young women who are not yet married"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "concerning the virgins, a command of [the] Lord I do not have" | parthenon as "virgins" |
| LOGOS | "Now concerning the betrothed, I have no command from the Lord" | parthenon as "betrothed" |
| PNEUMA | "Now regarding your question about the young women who are not yet married" | parthenon as "young women who are not yet married" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Betrothed" interprets parthenon in context (Paul is discussing whether engaged couples should marry). EMET's "virgins" is literal but in modern English connotes sexual status more than marital status. PNEUMA's expansion is too lengthy for a term Paul uses concisely. The rendering of parthenos is debated throughout vv.25-38 -- whether it means "virgin," "betrothed woman," or "fiancee."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The parthenos debate acknowledged.

---

## 7:26 -- "present distress / present crisis"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I consider then this to be good because of the present distress" | enestosan anagken as "present distress" |
| LOGOS | "I think that in view of the present distress it is good for a person to remain as he is" | Same term |
| PNEUMA | "Because of the present crisis, I think it is best to remain as you are" | anagke as "crisis"; "best" for kalos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Distress" for anagke is more accurate than "crisis" (PNEUMA), as anagke means necessity, compulsion, or distress -- not necessarily a crisis in the modern sense. Whether Paul refers to an immediate situation in Corinth or the general eschatological condition is debated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:29 -- "the appointed time has grown very short / the time that remains is very short / the time has been shortened"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the time has been shortened" | ho kairos synestalmenos estin -- perfect passive "has been shortened" |
| LOGOS | "the appointed time has grown very short" | Adds "appointed"; "grown very short" interprets the passive |
| PNEUMA | "the time that remains is very short" | "time that remains" adds "remains"; drops passive force |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The appointed time has grown very short" captures both the eschatological content (kairos as appointed/decisive time, not mere chronos) and the perfect passive (the shortening has already occurred and remains in effect). EMET's "has been shortened" preserves the passive but is less natural. PNEUMA's "the time that remains" adds a concept not in the Greek and loses the passive agency (God shortened the time).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:31 -- "present form / the world as we know it"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the form of this world is passing away" | schema tou kosmou as "form of this world" |
| LOGOS | "the present form of this world is passing away" | Adds "present" |
| PNEUMA | "this world as we know it will soon pass away" | Adds "as we know it" and "soon" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The present form (schema) of this world" accurately renders Paul's language -- it is the current configuration of the world, not the world itself, that is passing away. PNEUMA's "the world as we know it" is a reasonable paraphrase but "soon" adds temporal urgency not in the Greek (the verb is present tense: "is passing away," an ongoing process).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:36-38 -- "betrothed / virgin / fiancee" and the identity of the man

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone thinks he is behaving improperly toward the virgin of him, if she is past the bloom [of youth]...to keep the own virgin...the [one] giving in marriage does well" | parthenon autou as "the virgin of him"; gamizo as "giving in marriage" (father giving daughter) |
| LOGOS | "If anyone thinks that he is not behaving properly toward his betrothed...to keep her as his betrothed...he who marries his betrothed does well, and he who refrains from marriage will do even better" | parthenon as "betrothed"; gamizo as "marries" |
| PNEUMA | "if a man thinks that he's treating his fiancee improperly and will inevitably give in to his passion...So the person who marries does well, and the person who doesn't marry does even better" | parthenon as "fiancee"; adds "inevitably give in to his passion"; generalizes in v.38 |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. These verses are among the most debated in the chapter. Three interpretations exist: (a) a father deciding about his virgin daughter's marriage (EMET's gamizo, "giving in marriage," supports this); (b) a man and his fiancee (LOGOS and PNEUMA); (c) a spiritual marriage (celibate couple). SOPHIA follows LOGOS in reading (b) as most probable in context. PNEUMA's "will inevitably give in to his passion" adds words not in the Greek and over-specifies what hyperakmos means ("past the bloom" or "with strong passions").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The three-interpretation debate noted per EMET.

---

## 7:39 -- "falls asleep / dies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if the husband falls asleep, she is free to be married to whom she wishes, only in [the] Lord" | koimaomai as "falls asleep" (euphemism preserved) |
| LOGOS | "But if her husband dies, she is free to be married to whom she wishes, only in the Lord" | koimaomai as "dies" (euphemism decoded) |
| PNEUMA | "If her husband dies, she is free to marry anyone she wishes, but only if he loves the Lord" | "anyone"; "but only if he loves the Lord" interprets "only in the Lord" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Dies" correctly decodes the koimaomai ("falls asleep") euphemism for English readers. EMET's preservation of the euphemism is literal but potentially confusing. PNEUMA's "but only if he loves the Lord" is a significant interpretive expansion of "only in the Lord" (monon en kyrio), which could mean "only to a fellow believer" or "only in a manner consistent with the Lord's will." PNEUMA's rendering restricts it to the first interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:40 -- "happier / more blessed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she is more blessed if she remains thus, according to the judgment of me" | makarioteros as "more blessed" |
| LOGOS | "in my judgment she is happier if she remains as she is" | makarioteros as "happier" |
| PNEUMA | "in my opinion it would be better for her to stay single" | Drops makarioteros entirely; replaces with "better" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Happier" is an acceptable rendering of makarioteros (comparative of makarios, "blessed/happy"). PNEUMA's "better" loses the specific blessing/happiness language entirely. EMET's "more blessed" is more literal but somewhat formal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA removes or obscures quotation marks for Corinthian slogans | v.1 | Presents Corinthian position as Paul's own |
| PNEUMA conflates distinct Greek verbs | vv.10-11 | chorizo and aphiemi both rendered "leave" |
| PNEUMA restructures grammatical subject/object | v.14 | Unbeliever's passive sanctification becomes believer's active influence |
| PNEUMA over-specifies ambiguous Greek | vv.36-38, 39 | hyperakmos, "only in the Lord" |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Throughout | LOGOS as base text |
| EMET preserves euphemisms and ambiguities | vv.1, 21, 39 | "touch," mallon chresai, "falls asleep" |
| Key lexical debates | vv.3, 9, 15, 25 | opheile, pyrousthai, douloo vs. deo, parthenos |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 7 is Paul's longest and most nuanced chapter in this letter, dealing with multiple marriage-related situations. The most consequential translation divergence is PNEUMA's handling of v.1, where removing the quotation marks around the Corinthian slogan fundamentally misrepresents Paul's argument -- it makes Paul appear to endorse celibacy as the ideal rather than responding to an ascetic position. PNEUMA's conflation of chorizo and aphiemi (vv.10-11) and restructuring of v.14 also significantly alter Paul's meaning. The chapter reveals that PNEUMA's dynamic approach is most problematic when handling Paul's careful rhetorical engagement with Corinthian positions, where the distinction between Paul's voice and the Corinthians' voice is crucial. LOGOS consistently preserves these distinctions.

---
